name: Infant speech distorts full names into 'Pip'
source_quote: my infant tongue could make of both names nothing longer or more explicit than Pip.

name: Philip chooses to call himself Pip
source_quote: So, I, called myself Pip
time: After infancy speech develops enough to choose a name

name: Others begin calling Philip 'Pip'
source_quote: and came to be called Pip.
time: Early childhood, after he calls himself Pip

name: Philip reports his father’s family name as Pirrip
source_quote: I give Pirrip as my father’s family name

name: Philip relies on his father’s tombstone as authority for the name Pirrip
source_quote: on the authority of his tombstone
time: After his parents’ deaths, in Philip’s childhood

name: Philip relies on his sister’s word as authority for the name Pirrip
source_quote: and my sister,—Mrs. Joe Gargery

name: Mrs. Joe marries the blacksmith Joe Gargery
source_quote: my sister,—Mrs. Joe Gargery, who married the blacksmith.

name: Philip has never seen his father
source_quote: As I never saw my father
location: Throughout Philip’s life up to narration point

name: Philip has never seen his mother
source_quote: or my mother
location: Throughout Philip’s life up to narration point

name: Philip has never seen any likeness of his parents
source_quote: and never saw any likeness of either of them
location: Throughout Philip’s life up to narration point

name: Philip notes his parents lived long before photographs
source_quote: (for their days were long before the days of photographs)

name: Philip forms his first fancies about his parents’ appearance
source_quote: my first fancies regarding what they were like

name: Philip unreasonably derives parents’ appearance from their tombstones
source_quote: were unreasonably derived from their tombstones.

name: Philip observes the shape of the letters on his father’s tombstone
source_quote: The shape of the letters on my father’s

name: Philip gets an odd idea that his father was square, stout, dark with curly hair
source_quote: gave me an odd idea that he was a square, stout, dark man, with curly black hair.

name: Philip reads or notes the inscription 'Also Georgiana Wife of the Above'
source_quote: From the character and turn of the inscription, “Also Georgiana Wife of the Above,”
location: Graveyard, at mother’s portion of the tombstone

name: Philip draws a childish conclusion that his mother was freckled and sickly
source_quote: I drew a childish conclusion that my mother was freckled and sickly.

name: Five little stone lozenges are arranged in a neat row beside the parents’ grave
source_quote: To five little stone lozenges, each about a foot and a half long, which were arranged in a neat row beside their grave
location: Beside the grave of Philip’s parents, in the graveyard

name: The lozenges are made sacred to the memory of Philip’s five little brothers
source_quote: and were sacred to the memory of five little brothers of mine
time: At the time of the brothers’ burials

name: Five little brothers give up trying to get a living very early
source_quote: who gave up trying to get a living, exceedingly early in that universal struggle

name: Philip feels indebted to the stone lozenges for his belief about his brothers
source_quote: I am indebted for a belief I religiously entertained

name: Philip forms a belief that his brothers were born on their backs with their hands in their pockets
source_quote: that they had all been born on their backs with their hands in their trousers-pockets

name: Philip believes his brothers never took their hands out in this existence
source_quote: and had never taken them out in this state of existence.

name: Philip describes his home region as marsh country down by the river
source_quote: Ours was the marsh country, down by the river

name: Philip notes the river is within twenty miles of the sea as it winds
source_quote: within, as the river wound, twenty miles of the sea.
time: Based on geographic knowledge gained over time

name: Philip gains his first vivid and broad impression of the identity of things
source_quote: My first most vivid and broad impression of the identity of things seems to me to have been gained
time: On a memorable raw afternoon towards evening, in early life
location: Marsh country down by the river

name: Philip identifies the afternoon as memorable, raw, towards evening
source_quote: on a memorable raw afternoon towards evening.
time: That specific 'raw afternoon towards evening' in his childhood
location: Marsh country down by the river

name: Narrator realizes place is a churchyard
source_quote: I found out for certain that this bleak place overgrown with nettles was the churchyard
location: bleak place overgrown with nettles / churchyard

name: Narrator realizes Philip Pirrip is dead and buried
source_quote: and that Philip Pirrip, late of this parish... were dead and buried

name: Narrator realizes Georgiana is dead and buried
source_quote: and also Georgiana wife of the above, were dead and buried

name: Narrator realizes Alexander, Bartholomew, Abraham, Tobias, and Roger are dead and buried
source_quote: Alexander, Bartholomew, Abraham, Tobias, and Roger... were also dead and buried

name: Narrator realizes the wilderness beyond is the marshes
source_quote: and that the dark flat wilderness beyond the churchyard... was the marshes
location: dark flat wilderness beyond the churchyard

name: Narrator notices scattered cattle feeding on marshes
source_quote: with scattered cattle feeding on it

name: Narrator realizes the low leaden line is the river
source_quote: and that the low leaden line beyond was the river
location: low leaden line beyond the marshes

name: Narrator realizes the distant lair is the sea
source_quote: and that the distant savage lair from which the wind was rushing was the sea

name: Pip feels fear and begins to cry
source_quote: and that the small bundle of shivers growing afraid of it all and beginning to cry, was Pip

name: Pip self-identifies as the crying child
source_quote: the small bundle of shivers... was Pip

name: Man starts up from among the graves
source_quote: as a man started up from among the graves at the side of the church porch
location: among the graves at the side of the church porch

name: Man cries out to Pip to hold his noise
source_quote: “Hold your noise!” cried a terrible voice
time: Immediately after the man starts up

name: Man orders Pip to keep still and threatens to cut his throat
source_quote: “Keep still, you little devil, or I’ll cut your throat!”

name: Description establishes man’s coarse grey clothing and iron on leg
source_quote: A fearful man, all in coarse grey, with a great iron on his leg

name: Description notes man has no hat, broken shoes, rag on head
source_quote: A man with no hat, and with broken shoes, and with an old rag tied round his head

name: Description notes man has been soaked, smothered, lamed, cut, stung, torn
source_quote: A man who had been soaked in water, and smothered in mud, and lamed by stones, and cut by flints, and stung by nettles, and torn by briars

name: Man limps, shivers, glares, growls, and teeth chatter
source_quote: who limped, and shivered, and glared, and growled; and whose teeth chattered in his head

name: Man seizes Pip by the chin
source_quote: as he seized me by the chin

name: Pip pleads not to have his throat cut
source_quote: “Oh! Don’t cut my throat, sir,” I pleaded in terror. “Pray don’t do it, sir.”

name: Pip experiences terror while pleading
source_quote: I pleaded in terror

name: Man stares at Pip
source_quote: “Once more,” said the man, staring at me.

name: Man commands Pip to "Give it mouth"
source_quote: “Once more,” said the man... “Give it mouth!”

name: Man orders the boy to show where he lives
source_quote: “Show us where you live,” said the man. “Pint out the place!”
location: Unspecified outdoor area near village and church

name: Boy points toward the village location
source_quote: I pointed to where our village lay, on the flat in-shore among the alder-trees and pollards
time: Immediately after being ordered by the man
location: Flat in-shore among the alder-trees and pollards

name: Narrator describes village’s position relative to church
source_quote: on the flat in-shore among the alder-trees and pollards, a mile or more from the church.
time: Same moment as pointing, narrated retrospectively
location: Flat in-shore among the alder-trees and pollards, a mile or more from the church

name: Man looks at the boy
source_quote: The man, after looking at me for a moment

name: Man turns the boy upside down
source_quote: turned me upside down
time: Immediately after looking at the boy

name: Man empties the boy’s pockets
source_quote: and emptied my pockets.
time: Immediately after turning the boy upside down

name: Narrator notes pockets contain only a piece of bread
source_quote: There was nothing in them but a piece of bread.
time: At the moment the pockets are emptied, narrated retrospectively

name: Church seems to go head over heels in the boy’s perception
source_quote: he made it go head over heels before me, and I saw the steeple under my feet
time: While the man suddenly inverts the boy
location: Visual field of the narrator near the churchyard

name: Narrator sits on a high tombstone
source_quote: I was seated on a high tombstone
time: After the inversion, when 'the church came to itself'
location: On a high tombstone in the churchyard

name: Narrator trembles on the tombstone
source_quote: I was seated on a high tombstone, trembling
time: While the man eats the bread
location: On a high tombstone in the churchyard

name: Man eats the bread ravenously
source_quote: while he ate the bread ravenously.
time: While the boy sits trembling on the tombstone
location: Near the high tombstone in the churchyard

name: Man calls the boy a young dog and comments on his cheeks
source_quote: “You young dog,” said the man, licking his lips, “what fat cheeks you ha’ got.”
time: After or while eating the bread
location: Near the high tombstone in the churchyard

name: Man licks his lips
source_quote: said the man, licking his lips
time: As he speaks about the boy’s cheeks
location: Near the high tombstone in the churchyard

name: Narrator believes his cheeks were fat
source_quote: I believe they were fat
time: Narrative past reflection on that time of life
location: Narrative reflection (no specific physical location given)

name: Narrator states he was undersized for his years
source_quote: though I was at that time undersized for my years
time: At that time of his childhood, described from later

name: Narrator states he was not strong
source_quote: and not strong.

name: Man threatens he could eat them
source_quote: “Darn me if I couldn’t eat ’em,” said the man, with a threatening shake of his head

name: Man shakes his head threateningly
source_quote: said the man, with a threatening shake of his head

name: Man adds he has half a mind to eat them
source_quote: “and if I han’t half a mind to’t!”

name: Narrator expresses hope the man will not eat them
source_quote: I earnestly expressed my hope that he wouldn’t

name: Narrator clings tighter to the tombstone
source_quote: and held tighter to the tombstone on which he had put me

name: Narrator attempts to keep self on the tombstone
source_quote: partly, to keep myself upon it

name: Narrator tries to keep from crying
source_quote: partly, to keep myself from crying.

name: Man commands the child to look
source_quote: “Now lookee here!” said the man.
time: after the narrator grips the tombstone

name: Man asks where the child’s mother is
source_quote: “Where’s your mother?”

name: Man starts in reaction
source_quote: He started
time: immediately after asking about the mother

name: Man makes a short run
source_quote: made a short run

name: Man stops running
source_quote: and stopped

name: Man looks over his shoulder
source_quote: and stopped and looked over his shoulder.

name: Narrator timidly explains where his mother is
source_quote: “There, sir!” I timidly explained. “Also Georgiana. That’s my mother.”
time: after man looks over his shoulder

name: Narrator indicates the place of his mother with 'There, sir!'
source_quote: “There, sir!” I timidly explained.

name: Narrator identifies his mother as Georgiana
source_quote: “Also Georgiana. That’s my mother.”

name: Man returns to Pip
source_quote: “Oh!” said he, coming back.

name: Man exclaims 'Oh!'
source_quote: “Oh!” said he, coming back.

name: Man asks if the two figures are Pip's parents
source_quote: “And is that your father alonger your mother?”

name: Pip confirms the people are his parents
source_quote: “Yes, sir,” said I; “him too; late of this parish.”

name: Pip indicates his parents are deceased and from this parish
source_quote: “him too; late of this parish.”

name: Man mutters 'Ha!'
source_quote: “Ha!” he muttered then, considering.
time: after hearing that Pip's parents are dead

name: Man considers information about Pip
source_quote: he muttered then, considering.

name: Man asks who Pip lives with
source_quote: “Who d’ye live with,—supposin’ you’re kindly let to live, which I han’t made up my mind about?”

name: Man threatens Pip’s life conditionally
source_quote: “supposin’ you’re kindly let to live, which I han’t made up my mind about?”
time: within the same question about who Pip lives with

name: Pip states he lives with his sister
source_quote: “My sister, sir,—Mrs. Joe Gargery,—wife of Joe Gargery, the blacksmith, sir.”

name: Pip identifies his sister specifically as Mrs. Joe Gargery
source_quote: “My sister, sir,—Mrs. Joe Gargery,—wife of Joe Gargery…

name: Pip states Joe Gargery is a blacksmith
source_quote: wife of Joe Gargery, the blacksmith, sir.”

name: Man repeats 'Blacksmith, eh?'
source_quote: “Blacksmith, eh?” said he.
time: immediately after learning Joe is a blacksmith

name: Man looks down at his leg
source_quote: And looked down at his leg.

name: Man looks darkly at his leg and at narrator repeatedly
source_quote: After darkly looking at his leg and me several times
time: after darkly looking, initial moment of scene

name: Man comes closer to tombstone
source_quote: he came closer to my tombstone

name: Man grabs narrator by both arms
source_quote: took me by both arms

name: Man tilts narrator back as far as he can hold him
source_quote: and tilted me back as far as he could hold me

name: Man’s eyes look powerfully down into narrator’s eyes
source_quote: his eyes looked most powerfully down into mine

name: Narrator’s eyes look helplessly up at man’s eyes
source_quote: and mine looked most helplessly up into his

name: Man verbally introduces the question of whether narrator is to live
source_quote: “Now lookee here,” he said, “the question being whether you’re to be let to live.

name: Man asks if narrator knows what a file is
source_quote: You know what a file is?

name: Man tilts narrator over a little more after each question
source_quote: After each question he tilted me over a little more

name: Narrator experiences greater sense of helplessness and danger
source_quote: so as to give me a greater sense of helplessness and danger
time: as he is tilted after each question

name: Man commands narrator to get him a file
source_quote: “You get me a file.”

name: Man tilts narrator again after demanding a file
source_quote: He tilted me again.
time: immediately after telling him to get a file

name: Man commands narrator to get him wittles (food)
source_quote: “And you get me wittles.”

name: Man tilts narrator again after demanding wittles
source_quote: He tilted me again.

name: Man orders narrator to bring both file and wittles to him
source_quote: “You bring ’em both to me.”
time: after individually demanding file and wittles

name: Man tilts narrator again after ordering him to bring both items
source_quote: He tilted me again.
time: immediately after command to bring both

name: Man threatens to take narrator’s heart and liver out
source_quote: “Or I’ll have your heart and liver out.”
time: after ordering narrator to bring file and wittles

name: Man tilts narrator again after issuing the violent threat
source_quote: He tilted me again.

name: Narrator feels dreadfully frightened
source_quote: I was dreadfully frightened

name: Narrator feels giddy
source_quote: and so giddy

name: Narrator clings to man with both hands
source_quote: that I clung to him with both hands

name: Narrator politely asks to be allowed to keep upright
source_quote: “If you would kindly please to let me keep upright, sir

name: Narrator states that remaining upright might prevent sickness
source_quote: perhaps I shouldn’t be sick
time: same utterance as request to keep upright

name: Narrator states that being upright might help him attend more
source_quote: and perhaps I could attend more.

name: Man violently dips and rolls narrator
source_quote: He gave me a most tremendous dip and roll, so that the church jumped over its own weathercock.
location: near the church, on top of the stone

name: Narrator perceives church seeming to jump
source_quote: so that the church jumped over its own weathercock.
location: near the church, on top of the stone

name: Man holds narrator upright by the arms
source_quote: Then, he held me by the arms, in an upright position on the top of the stone
time: immediately after the dip and roll
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man continues speaking in fearful terms
source_quote: and went on in these fearful terms:—
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man commands narrator to bring file and food next morning
source_quote: “You bring me, to-morrow morning early, that file and them wittles.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man specifies delivery location at old Battery
source_quote: You bring the lot to me, at that old Battery over yonder.
location: near the church, on the top of the stone (speaking about old Battery)

name: Man orders narrator to keep complete secrecy
source_quote: you never dare to say a word or dare to make a sign concerning your having seen such a person as me
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man promises narrator will be allowed to live if he obeys
source_quote: and you shall be let to live.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man threatens to tear out, roast, and eat boy’s organs if he fails
source_quote: your heart and your liver shall be tore out, roasted, and ate.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man asserts he is not alone
source_quote: Now, I ain’t alone, as you may think I am.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man claims existence of a hidden young man with him
source_quote: There’s a young man hid with me, in comparison with which young man I am a Angel.
location: on the top of the stone (young man said to be hidden nearby)

name: Man asserts young man hears his words
source_quote: That young man hears the words I speak.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man describes young man’s peculiar way of getting at a boy’s heart and liver
source_quote: That young man has a secret way pecooliar to himself, of getting at a boy, and at his heart, and at his liver.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man asserts futility of a boy hiding from young man
source_quote: It is in wain for a boy to attempt to hide himself from that young man.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man lists possible ways a boy might try to secure himself
source_quote: A boy may lock his door, may be warm in bed, may tuck himself up, may draw the clothes over his head
location: on the top of the stone (describing indoor scenes hypothetically)

name: Man claims young man will creep to the boy and tear him open
source_quote: but that young man will softly creep and creep his way to him and tear him open.

name: Man claims he is keeping young man from harming narrator
source_quote: I am a keeping that young man from harming of you at the present moment
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man claims it is very hard to restrain young man from narrator’s inside
source_quote: I find it wery hard to hold that young man off of your inside.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man demands narrator’s verbal response
source_quote: Now, what do you say?”
location: on the top of the stone

name: Narrator verbally agrees to get the file
source_quote: I said that I would get him the file
location: on the top of the stone

name: Narrator agrees to bring broken bits of food
source_quote: and I would get him what broken bits of food I could
location: on the top of the stone

name: Narrator promises to meet man at the Battery early in the morning
source_quote: and I would come to him at the Battery, early in the morning.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man orders narrator to swear an oath (“Lord strike you dead”)
source_quote: “Say Lord strike you dead if you don’t!” said the man.
location: on the top of the stone

name: Man reminds listener of his undertaking and orders him home
source_quote: “Now,” he pursued, “you remember what you’ve undertook, and you remember that young man, and you get home!”
location: unspecified outdoor location near the marshes

name: Man glances about over the cold wet flat
source_quote: “Much of that!” said he, glancing about him over the cold wet flat.

name: Man exclaims dismissively "Much of that!"
source_quote: “Much of that!” said he

name: Man expresses wish to be a frog
source_quote: “I wish I was a frog.

name: Man expresses wish to be an eel
source_quote: Or a eel!”
time: immediately after wishing to be a frog

name: Man hugs his shuddering body in both arms
source_quote: he hugged his shuddering body in both his arms,—clasping himself, as if to hold himself together,
time: at the same time as his exclamation

name: Man clasps himself as if to hold himself together
source_quote: —clasping himself, as if to hold himself together,—

name: Man limps towards the low church wall
source_quote: and limped towards the low church wall.
location: towards the low church wall across the marshy ground

name: Narrator sees the man going
source_quote: As I saw him go, picking his way among the nettles
time: as the man begins to move away

name: Man picks his way among the nettles
source_quote: picking his way among the nettles
time: as he moves toward the wall
location: ground near low church wall with nettles

name: Man picks his way among the brambles binding the green mounds
source_quote: and among the brambles that bound the green mounds
time: continuation of movement toward the wall
location: among brambles that bound the green mounds in the churchyard

name: Man looks to narrator like he is eluding dead people’s hands
source_quote: he looked in my young eyes as if he were eluding the hands of the dead people

name: Narrator imagines dead people stretching up cautiously from graves
source_quote: stretching up cautiously out of their graves, to get a twist upon his ankle and pull him in.
time: same moment as watching the man

name: Narrator imagines dead people trying to twist the man’s ankle and pull him in
source_quote: to get a twist upon his ankle and pull him in.

name: Man comes to the low church wall
source_quote: When he came to the low church wall

name: Man gets over the low church wall like a numbed and stiff man
source_quote: he got over it, like a man whose legs were numbed and stiff

name: Man turns round to look for the narrator
source_quote: and then turned round to look for me.
location: just beyond the low church wall

name: Narrator sees the man turning
source_quote: When I saw him turning

name: Narrator sets face towards home
source_quote: I set my face towards home

name: Narrator makes the best use of legs (runs or walks quickly)
source_quote: and made the best use of my legs.
location: path towards home from the churchyard

name: Narrator looks over shoulder while going home
source_quote: But presently I looked over my shoulder

name: Narrator sees man going on again towards the river
source_quote: and saw him going on again towards the river

name: Narrator perceives man still hugging himself in both arms
source_quote: still hugging himself in both arms

name: Narrator perceives man picking his way with sore feet among great stones
source_quote: and picking his way with his sore feet among the great stones
time: same observation as he goes to the river
location: marshes with great stones for stepping-places

name: Stones are used as stepping-places when rains are heavy or tide is in
source_quote: great stones dropped into the marshes here and there, for stepping-places when the rains were heavy or the tide was in.
time: whenever the rains are heavy or the tide is in

name: Narrator stops to look after the man
source_quote: The marshes were just a long black horizontal line then, as I stopped to look after him
time: as the marshes appear as a long black horizontal line
location: edge of marshes with view of river and sky

name: Narrator perceives marshes as a long black horizontal line
source_quote: The marshes were just a long black horizontal line then
time: at the moment narrator stops to look

name: Narrator perceives the river as another, narrower and less black line
source_quote: and the river was just another horizontal line, not nearly so broad nor yet so black

name: Narrator perceives the sky as a row of long angry red and dense black lines intermixed
source_quote: and the sky was just a row of long angry red lines and dense black lines intermixed.
time: same moment as viewing marshes and river

name: Narrator perceives two upright black things by the river
source_quote: On the edge of the river I could faintly make out the only two black things in all the prospect

name: Narrator recognizes beacon as one of the black things
source_quote: one of these was the beacon by which the sailors steered

name: Narrator describes beacon’s shape
source_quote: like an unhooped cask upon a pole

name: Narrator judges the beacon as ugly up close
source_quote: an ugly thing when you were near it
location: near the beacon by the river

name: Narrator identifies the second black thing as a gibbet
source_quote: the other, a gibbet

name: Narrator observes chains hanging from the gibbet
source_quote: with some chains hanging to it
location: at the gibbet by the river

name: Narrator recalls chains once held a pirate
source_quote: which had once held a pirate
time: past relative to narration ("had once held")
location: at the gibbet by the river

name: Man limps toward the gibbet
source_quote: The man was limping on towards this latter
location: towards the gibbet by the river

name: Narrator imagines man as pirate come to life
source_quote: as if he were the pirate come to life, and come down
time: while man is limping toward gibbet
location: edge of the river, observing the gibbet

name: Narrator imagines man going back to hook himself up again
source_quote: and going back to hook himself up again
location: edge of the river, looking at gibbet

name: Narrator experiences a terrible turn (shock) at the thought
source_quote: It gave me a terrible turn when I thought so
time: immediately after imagining the man as pirate

name: Narrator sees cattle lifting their heads
source_quote: as I saw the cattle lifting their heads to gaze after him

name: Cattle lift their heads to gaze after the man
source_quote: the cattle lifting their heads to gaze after him

name: Narrator wonders if cattle share his eerie thought
source_quote: I wondered whether they thought so too
time: immediately after seeing cattle gaze after him

name: Narrator looks all round for the horrible young man
source_quote: I looked all round for the horrible young man
location: edge of the river / surrounding area

name: Narrator fails to see any signs of the horrible young man
source_quote: and could see no signs of him
location: edge of the river / surrounding prospect

name: Narrator becomes frightened again
source_quote: But now I was frightened again
time: after failing to see the horrible young man

name: Narrator runs home without stopping
source_quote: and ran home without stopping
location: from edge of the river / marshes to home
